title: Dissipation Mechanisms and Dissipative Phase Transitions of two coupled Fully Connected Quantum Ising models
authors: Bidyut Dey, Andrea Nava, Domenico Giuliano
abstract: We study dissipative phase transitions in a system of two coupled fully-connected quantum Ising models interacting with an environment. The dynamics is governed by a Lindblad master equation combining coherent unitary evolution and incoherent dissipative processes, where the unitary part is described within a self-consistent mean-field framework effectively acting on the local Hilbert space of two coupled spins at each site.   We analyze two fundamentally different classes of dissipators. In the first case, the jump operators are defined in the instantaneous eigenbasis of the mean-field Hamiltonian and satisfy a detailed-balance condition. In this setting, the relaxation dynamics depends strongly on the quench protocol: a parametric quench of the Hamiltonian leads to conventional relaxation, whereas a temperature quench gives rise to a dynamical phase transition characterized by nonanalytic behavior in time. Yet, in both cases, the system relaxes toward a steady state determined solely by the post-quench parameters and the bath temperature, which closely resembles a thermal Gibbs state of the mean-field Hamiltonian. As a result, the dissipative phase transition occurs at a critical point consistent with the corresponding equilibrium transition. In contrast, when the dissipators are realized via local spin raising and lowering operators, the steady state is genuinely nonequilibrium, leading to a significantly richer phase diagram. In particular, for sufficiently strong system-bath coupling, we observe a reentrant phase featuring a symmetry-broken region bounded by two continuous dissipative phase transitions.   Our results evidence how the structure of dissipative processes controls the emergence of equilibrium-like versus genuinely nonequilibrium critical behavior in open quantum systems.
summary: This paper explores how the nature of dissipation determines whether an open quantum system relaxes to a thermal-like state or a complex nonequilibrium state. By studying coupled quantum Ising models, the authors demonstrate that local dissipation can induce a unique reentrant phase transition. This work highlights the critical role of dissipative mechanisms in shaping the phase diagrams of many-body systems.
main_problem: Investigating how different dissipative mechanisms (detailed balance vs. local pump-loss) control the emergence of equilibrium-like versus genuinely nonequilibrium critical behavior and phase transitions in open quantum systems.
main_result: The study finds that while detailed-balance dissipation leads to steady states resembling thermal Gibbs states, local spin-operator dissipation creates a richer nonequilibrium phase diagram featuring a reentrant symmetry-broken phase.
method: The authors use a self-consistent mean-field approach to solve a nonlinear Lindblad master equation, employing linear stability analysis and numerical integration to characterize phase transitions and relaxation dynamics.
model_or_system: Two coupled, fully connected (all-to-all) quantum Ising models governed by a nonlinear Lindblad master equation that includes both coherent unitary evolution and incoherent dissipative processes.
key_observables: Magnetization (order parameter), inter-model correlation functions, mixed-state fidelity, fidelity rate function, and quantum Fisher information.
important_parameters: Transverse field strengths (h1, h2), inter-model coupling (lambda), bath temperature (T), and system-bath coupling strength (gamma).
main_assumptions: The system is treated in the thermodynamic limit using a mean-field approximation, and the environment is assumed to be Markovian (memoryless).
figures_summary: Figures show post-quench fidelity and magnetization dynamics, the phase diagram in the h1-gamma plane, the stability gap, and bifurcation diagrams illustrating the emergence of symmetry-broken phases.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the model and dissipative classes, analyzes relaxation dynamics following parameter quenches, explores steady-state phase diagrams for different dissipators, and concludes with a comparison of equilibrium-like vs. nonequilibrium criticality.
why_interesting: It provides fundamental insights into how the specific structure of system-environment coupling can qualitatively change the phase structure and critical behavior of many-body open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Phase diagram of the fully connected quantum Ising model in Eq. (5). The black curve denotes the critical tem- perature given by Eq. (9). Below this line the system is in the ordered phase (OP) with spontaneous Z2 symmetry breaking, while above it the system is in the disordered phase (DP).

FIG. 5. Top: mx,1(t) vs tγ. Bottom: O(t) vs tγ. In both panels, we consider a temperature quench Ti = 0.4 →Tf = 1.05 at fixed h1 = 0.6. The blue and red curves correspond to γ = 0.02 and γ = 0.2, respectively. Other parameters are h2 = 10, λ = 0.01, and r = 20. The black vertical dashed line indicates the location of the dynamical phase transition. Inset (Bottom panel): O(t) vs tγ in the vicinity of the dynamical phase transition.

FIG. 6. Rate function r(t) as a function of the rescaled time tγ for a temperature quench Ti = 0.4 →Tf = 1.05 at fixed h1 = 0.6. The blue and red curves correspond to γ = 0.02 and γ = 0.2, respectively. Other parameters are h2 = 10, λ = 0.01, and r = 20. The black vertical dashed line marks the location of the dynamical phase transition.

Figure 6 displays the corresponding dynamics of r(t) for the same quench protocol as in Fig. 5. For weak dissipa- tion (γ = 0.02, blue curve), the rate function exhibits a distinct nonanalyticity at a critical time t∗(scaled by γ), signaling a dynamical phase transition. In contrast, for stronger dissipation, the nonanalytic feature is smoothed out, and the dynamics display a continuous crossover. Unlike the transverse-field quench, here, nonanalytic be- havior in the time evolution of observables persists in the presence of dissipation. However, increasing the dis- sipation strength progressively smears out the dynamical phase transition [67]. In conclusion, our results demonstrate that,...

FIG. 7. Steady state magnetization m1,ss (left) and inter- model correlator Oss (right) for forward (dashed blue) and backward sweep (dashed red) of h1. The black vertical line indicates the transition line at h1,c = 1. We keep bath tem- perature fixed at T = 0.05.

Partially Pumped Collective Emitters.note: The paper's central investigation into how local spin-operator dissipation (as opposed to global/detailed-balance dissipation) creates unique non-equilibrium phase diagrams directly overlaps with the physics of inhomogeneous or local driving in ensembles.
